title: Polycystic Kidney Disease (ADPKD)
oneLiner: Autosomal dominant disorder causing progressive renal cyst growth; leads to hypertension, pain, infection, and end-stage renal disease (ESRD) by age 50–60.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Ruptured intracranial aneurysm (ICA):** sudden thunderclap headache ± neck stiffness, focal neurology, reduced GCS → requires urgent CT head ± LP
- **Cyst haemorrhage with obstructive clot:** sudden flank pain + visible haematuria + anuria/oliguria → bedside bladder scan shows distended bladder → needs catheter ± urgent urology (clot retention can cause acute kidney injury)
- **Cyst infection with sepsis:** fever + rigors + flank tenderness + high NEWS2 (≥5) → blood cultures, IV antibiotics, fluid resuscitation – do not delay antibiotics for imaging
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Bilateral flank/abdominal pain (dull ache from enlarged kidneys or acute sharp pain from cyst rupture/haemorrhage)
  - Visible haematuria (often after minor trauma or physical activity)
  - Hypertension (very common, often early)
  - Recurrent UTIs (cyst infection often mimics pyelonephritis)
  - Palpable abdominal masses (enlarged kidneys on exam)
  - Headache (check BP, also consider ICA if sudden/severe)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Abdominal palpation: bilateral, irregular, firm kidneys (may be massive)
  - Hypertension (often high diastolic)
  - Signs of chronic kidney disease: pallor, oedema, uraemic fetor (late)
  - Neurological: if ICA suspected – focal signs, neck stiffness
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely:** Simple renal cysts (incidental, no family history, no hypertension, <3 cysts per kidney) → USS shows thin-walled, simple cysts; no family history; normal BP.
- **Less likely:**
  - Autosomal recessive PKD (ARPKD) – presents in childhood, hepatomegaly, no family history of dominant pattern.
  - Tuberous sclerosis complex (angiomyolipomas, seizures, skin lesions).
  - Von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) – renal cell carcinoma, retinal/cerebellar haemangioblastomas.
  - Acquired cystic kidney disease (in long-term dialysis patients).
- **Splitter:** Family history of ADPKD + bilateral kidney enlargement + >10 cysts per kidney on USS (especially in a young adult) → ADPKD vs simple cysts.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine dipstick: blood, protein, leucocytes, nitrites (infection)
  - BP measurement (both arms)
  - ECG if hypertensive or suspected ICA (LVH, arrhythmia)
  - Bladder scan if anuric with visible haematuria
- **Bloods:**
  - U&Es, creatinine (eGFR), LFTs, FBC, CRP
  - Blood cultures if febrile
  - Coagulation screen (if planned for intervention or ICA)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Renal ultrasound** – diagnostic for ADPKD (bilateral enlarged kidneys with multiple cysts); also screen for stones, infection
  - Second-line: **CT abdomen (non-contrast)** for acute pain/haematuria (identifies haemorrhagic cysts, stones, obstruction); **CT with contrast** if suspect tumour or infection (cyst wall enhancement)
  - **MRI abdomen** if contrast contraindicated (e.g. eGFR <30) – better for cyst morphology and detecting complicated cysts
  - **Head imaging:** If thunderclap headache – urgent **CT head** (non-contrast) ± **CT angiogram** if subarachnoid haemorrhage suspected
- **Special tests:**
  - Genetic testing (PKD1/PKD2) – usually for atypical cases or family planning
  - Urine culture if infection suspected
  - Renal biopsy rarely needed (only if atypical presentation)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **Pei-Ravine criteria** (ultrasound-based): For patients aged 15–39: ≥3 unilateral or ≥2 bilateral cysts; 40–59: ≥2 cysts per kidney; ≥60: ≥4 cysts per kidney. (Positive family history usually sufficient.)
- **Never-miss test:** **Urine culture** before antibiotics in suspected cyst infection; **CT head** in any sudden severe headache to exclude aneurysm rupture.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If suspected **ruptured ICA**: urgent CT head, neurosurgery referral, secure airway if GCS <8, BP control (target SBP <160 mmHg with labetalol or nimodipine – per local protocol)
  - If **cyst infection**: IV fluids, IV antibiotics (e.g. ciprofloxacin or TMP-SMX – cover gram-negative; adjust for renal function; duration 2-4 weeks)
  - If **cyst haemorrhage with clot retention**: catheterise (wide-bore 3-way if needed), bladder irrigation, urology review
  - **Analgesia**: paracetamol (avoid NSAIDs if eGFR <30 or hypertensive – risk of AKI). For severe pain consider morphine IV (low dose, monitor renal function)
  - **BP management**: if hypertensive crisis (BP >180/120), consider IV labetalol or hydralazine per local protocol
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Monitor urine output, BP, NEWS2
  - IV antibiotics until afebrile 48h, then switch to oral
  - Ensure adequate hydration (oral or IV) – cyst expansion may be worsened by dehydration? No strong evidence, but avoid hypovolaemia
  - Catheter care if indwelling – remove as soon as possible to reduce infection risk
  - Pain control – stepwise (paracetamol ± codeine; avoid NSAIDs)
  - Refer to nephrology if eGFR <60 or rapidly declining
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Hypertension**: first-line ACE inhibitor (e.g. ramipril 2.5–10mg daily) or ARB (e.g. losartan) – reduces progression and cardiovascular risk. Target BP <130/80 (or <120/80 if significant proteinuria)
  - **Tolvaptan** (vasopressin V2 receptor antagonist) – specialist initiation only for rapidly progressing ADPKD (eGFR 25–89, age <55, with evidence of fast decline). Must monitor LFTs – risk of hepatotoxicity
  - Lifestyle: low-salt diet (<5g/day), maintain hydration (2–3L/day unless advanced CKD or fluid overload), avoid smoking, regular exercise
  - Screen for **intracranial aneurysms** – if family history of ICA or high-risk (e.g. PKD1 mutation, smoking, hypertension) → MRA head every 2-5 years
  - Vaccinations: annual influenza, pneumococcal, hepatitis B (if CKD stage 4-5)
  - Monitor eGFR, proteinuria, BP every 6-12 months
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% saline at maintenance (e.g. 1L/8h) unless fluid overloaded or on dialysis
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 20–40mg SC once daily (adjust for renal impairment – use 20mg if eGFR <30)
  - Diet: normal unless hyperkalaemia or severe CKD (then renal diet)
  - Observation: NEWS2 every 4h (more frequent if sepsis or instability)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Ramipril 2.5mg PO once daily, titrate up to 10mg daily (target BP <130/80). Check U&Es and creatinine within 2 weeks.
  - Ciprofloxacin 500mg PO BD (or 400mg IV BD) – for cyst infection. Duration: 14–21 days. Adjust dose if eGFR <30: 250mg PO BD.
  - Tolvaptan (if specialist decision) – start 45mg PO AM + 15mg PM, titrate to 60mg/30mg, then 90mg/30mg. Monitor LFTs at baseline, 2 weeks, then monthly for 18 months.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Losartan 25–50mg PO daily (if ACE-I intolerant)
  - Doxycycline 200mg loading then 100mg PO/IV BD (for cyst infection if contraindication to quinolones)
  - Co-trimoxazole (TMP-SMX) 960mg PO/IV BD (but reserve for proven susceptibility; risk of hyperkalaemia)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - ACE-I + NSAIDs → high risk of AKI and hyperkalaemia
  - Tolvaptan + strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (e.g. clarithromycin, ketoconazole) – contraindicated
  - Ciprofloxacin: avoid with tizanidine; risk of QT prolongation with class III antiarrhythmics
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - ACE-I/ARB: no dose adjustment needed in CKD; monitor K+ and creatinine
  - Ciprofloxacin: reduce dose if eGFR <30 (e.g. 250mg PO BD)
  - Tolvaptan: avoid if eGFR <25 (not effective)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Tolvaptan** – starting without checking LFTs or missing monitoring → risk of acute liver failure. **Ciprofloxacin** – prescribing full dose in severe renal impairment without eGFR check → neurotoxicity.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Cyst haemorrhage (pain, haematuria, clot retention)
  - Cyst infection (fever, flank pain, sepsis)
  - Ruptured intracranial aneurysm (subarachnoid haemorrhage)
  - Acute hypertension (can be severe)
  - Obstructive uropathy (rare, from large cysts compressing ureter)
- **Chronic:**
  - Progressive CKD → ESRD requiring RRT (dialysis/transplant) – median age 58 (PKD1) vs 79 (PKD2)
  - Hypertension – early and often severe
  - Chronic pain – from cyst expansion, pressure
  - Recurrent UTIs
  - Hepatic cysts (co-existent with renal cysts in >80% by age 60)
  - Cardiac valvular abnormalities (mitral valve prolapse)
  - Colonic diverticula (incidence increased)
  - Abdominal wall hernias (from increased intra-abdominal pressure)
sections.Prognosis: - Median age at ESRD: PKD1 mutation ~58 years; PKD2 mutation ~79 years. Progression slowed by BP control and tolvaptan (if eligible). Life expectancy reduced vs general population (cardiovascular disease main cause of death pre-ESRD; infection/sepsis after RRT).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Start an ACE-I or ARB early** (at first sign of hypertension or microalbuminuria) – renoprotective and reduces left ventricular hypertrophy.
- **Always ask about family history** of brain aneurysm – if positive or personal high risk, refer for screening MRA.
- **Maintain high hydration** (2–3L/day) – reduces vasopressin levels, may slow cyst growth (especially in early-stage disease before eGFR decline).
- **In suspected cyst infection:** treat for 2-4 weeks (longer than standard pyelonephritis) – abscesses often require drainage.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT give NSAIDs** for pain in ADPKD – they reduce renal blood flow and can precipitate AKI, especially in CKD.
- **Do NOT dismiss new severe headache** as “just a migraine” – always CT head to exclude ICA rupture. ADPKD has 5-10% prevalence of IA.
- **Do NOT delay antibiotics** for cyst infection while waiting for imaging – sepsis can progress rapidly. Start empirical IV antibiotics after blood cultures.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Sudden severe headache (worst ever) – call 999
  - Fever >38°C with flank pain – possible cyst infection
  - Visible haematuria with inability to pass urine – clot retention
  - Acute severe flank pain that does not settle – re-evaluate for haemorrhage/stones
  - New confusion, seizure, or focal weakness – ?intracranial bleed
- **Follow-up:** Nephrology clinic within 4-6 weeks for stable patients; routine monitoring BP/eGFR every 6 months. If new diagnosis, refer to renal genetics if considering family screening.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially with fever, hypotension)
  - Sudden GCS drop <13 or seizure (suspected ICA rupture)
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L (sepsis)
  - eGFR decline >30% in 48h (acute on chronic kidney injury)
  - Oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/h for >6h) despite fluid resuscitation
  - Unable to control BP (SBP >180 despite oral therapy)
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 → immediate senior review
  - GCS <8 → call ICU for airway support
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L → sepsis / shock → involve critical care
  - Anuria for >12h → urgent urology and nephrology

rawMarkdown:
## Polycystic Kidney Disease (ADPKD)
**One-liner:** Autosomal dominant disorder causing progressive renal cyst growth; leads to hypertension, pain, infection, and end-stage renal disease (ESRD) by age 50–60.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Ruptured intracranial aneurysm (ICA):** sudden thunderclap headache ± neck stiffness, focal neurology, reduced GCS → requires urgent CT head ± LP
- **Cyst haemorrhage with obstructive clot:** sudden flank pain + visible haematuria + anuria/oliguria → bedside bladder scan shows distended bladder → needs catheter ± urgent urology (clot retention can cause acute kidney injury)
- **Cyst infection with sepsis:** fever + rigors + flank tenderness + high NEWS2 (≥5) → blood cultures, IV antibiotics, fluid resuscitation – do not delay antibiotics for imaging

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Bilateral flank/abdominal pain (dull ache from enlarged kidneys or acute sharp pain from cyst rupture/haemorrhage)
  - Visible haematuria (often after minor trauma or physical activity)
  - Hypertension (very common, often early)
  - Recurrent UTIs (cyst infection often mimics pyelonephritis)
  - Palpable abdominal masses (enlarged kidneys on exam)
  - Headache (check BP, also consider ICA if sudden/severe)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Abdominal palpation: bilateral, irregular, firm kidneys (may be massive)
  - Hypertension (often high diastolic)
  - Signs of chronic kidney disease: pallor, oedema, uraemic fetor (late)
  - Neurological: if ICA suspected – focal signs, neck stiffness

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely:** Simple renal cysts (incidental, no family history, no hypertension, <3 cysts per kidney) → USS shows thin-walled, simple cysts; no family history; normal BP.
- **Less likely:**
  - Autosomal recessive PKD (ARPKD) – presents in childhood, hepatomegaly, no family history of dominant pattern.
  - Tuberous sclerosis complex (angiomyolipomas, seizures, skin lesions).
  - Von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) – renal cell carcinoma, retinal/cerebellar haemangioblastomas.
  - Acquired cystic kidney disease (in long-term dialysis patients).
- **Splitter:** Family history of ADPKD + bilateral kidney enlargement + >10 cysts per kidney on USS (especially in a young adult) → ADPKD vs simple cysts.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine dipstick: blood, protein, leucocytes, nitrites (infection)
  - BP measurement (both arms)
  - ECG if hypertensive or suspected ICA (LVH, arrhythmia)
  - Bladder scan if anuric with visible haematuria
- **Bloods:**
  - U&Es, creatinine (eGFR), LFTs, FBC, CRP
  - Blood cultures if febrile
  - Coagulation screen (if planned for intervention or ICA)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Renal ultrasound** – diagnostic for ADPKD (bilateral enlarged kidneys with multiple cysts); also screen for stones, infection
  - Second-line: **CT abdomen (non-contrast)** for acute pain/haematuria (identifies haemorrhagic cysts, stones, obstruction); **CT with contrast** if suspect tumour or infection (cyst wall enhancement)
  - **MRI abdomen** if contrast contraindicated (e.g. eGFR <30) – better for cyst morphology and detecting complicated cysts
  - **Head imaging:** If thunderclap headache – urgent **CT head** (non-contrast) ± **CT angiogram** if subarachnoid haemorrhage suspected
- **Special tests:**
  - Genetic testing (PKD1/PKD2) – usually for atypical cases or family planning
  - Urine culture if infection suspected
  - Renal biopsy rarely needed (only if atypical presentation)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **Pei-Ravine criteria** (ultrasound-based): For patients aged 15–39: ≥3 unilateral or ≥2 bilateral cysts; 40–59: ≥2 cysts per kidney; ≥60: ≥4 cysts per kidney. (Positive family history usually sufficient.)
- **Never-miss test:** **Urine culture** before antibiotics in suspected cyst infection; **CT head** in any sudden severe headache to exclude aneurysm rupture.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If suspected **ruptured ICA**: urgent CT head, neurosurgery referral, secure airway if GCS <8, BP control (target SBP <160 mmHg with labetalol or nimodipine – per local protocol)
  - If **cyst infection**: IV fluids, IV antibiotics (e.g. ciprofloxacin or TMP-SMX – cover gram-negative; adjust for renal function; duration 2-4 weeks)
  - If **cyst haemorrhage with clot retention**: catheterise (wide-bore 3-way if needed), bladder irrigation, urology review
  - **Analgesia**: paracetamol (avoid NSAIDs if eGFR <30 or hypertensive – risk of AKI). For severe pain consider morphine IV (low dose, monitor renal function)
  - **BP management**: if hypertensive crisis (BP >180/120), consider IV labetalol or hydralazine per local protocol
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Monitor urine output, BP, NEWS2
  - IV antibiotics until afebrile 48h, then switch to oral
  - Ensure adequate hydration (oral or IV) – cyst expansion may be worsened by dehydration? No strong evidence, but avoid hypovolaemia
  - Catheter care if indwelling – remove as soon as possible to reduce infection risk
  - Pain control – stepwise (paracetamol ± codeine; avoid NSAIDs)
  - Refer to nephrology if eGFR <60 or rapidly declining
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Hypertension**: first-line ACE inhibitor (e.g. ramipril 2.5–10mg daily) or ARB (e.g. losartan) – reduces progression and cardiovascular risk. Target BP <130/80 (or <120/80 if significant proteinuria)
  - **Tolvaptan** (vasopressin V2 receptor antagonist) – specialist initiation only for rapidly progressing ADPKD (eGFR 25–89, age <55, with evidence of fast decline). Must monitor LFTs – risk of hepatotoxicity
  - Lifestyle: low-salt diet (<5g/day), maintain hydration (2–3L/day unless advanced CKD or fluid overload), avoid smoking, regular exercise
  - Screen for **intracranial aneurysms** – if family history of ICA or high-risk (e.g. PKD1 mutation, smoking, hypertension) → MRA head every 2-5 years
  - Vaccinations: annual influenza, pneumococcal, hepatitis B (if CKD stage 4-5)
  - Monitor eGFR, proteinuria, BP every 6-12 months
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% saline at maintenance (e.g. 1L/8h) unless fluid overloaded or on dialysis
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 20–40mg SC once daily (adjust for renal impairment – use 20mg if eGFR <30)
  - Diet: normal unless hyperkalaemia or severe CKD (then renal diet)
  - Observation: NEWS2 every 4h (more frequent if sepsis or instability)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Ramipril 2.5mg PO once daily, titrate up to 10mg daily (target BP <130/80). Check U&Es and creatinine within 2 weeks.
  - Ciprofloxacin 500mg PO BD (or 400mg IV BD) – for cyst infection. Duration: 14–21 days. Adjust dose if eGFR <30: 250mg PO BD.
  - Tolvaptan (if specialist decision) – start 45mg PO AM + 15mg PM, titrate to 60mg/30mg, then 90mg/30mg. Monitor LFTs at baseline, 2 weeks, then monthly for 18 months.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Losartan 25–50mg PO daily (if ACE-I intolerant)
  - Doxycycline 200mg loading then 100mg PO/IV BD (for cyst infection if contraindication to quinolones)
  - Co-trimoxazole (TMP-SMX) 960mg PO/IV BD (but reserve for proven susceptibility; risk of hyperkalaemia)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - ACE-I + NSAIDs → high risk of AKI and hyperkalaemia
  - Tolvaptan + strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (e.g. clarithromycin, ketoconazole) – contraindicated
  - Ciprofloxacin: avoid with tizanidine; risk of QT prolongation with class III antiarrhythmics
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - ACE-I/ARB: no dose adjustment needed in CKD; monitor K+ and creatinine
  - Ciprofloxacin: reduce dose if eGFR <30 (e.g. 250mg PO BD)
  - Tolvaptan: avoid if eGFR <25 (not effective)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Tolvaptan** – starting without checking LFTs or missing monitoring → risk of acute liver failure. **Ciprofloxacin** – prescribing full dose in severe renal impairment without eGFR check → neurotoxicity.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Cyst haemorrhage (pain, haematuria, clot retention)
  - Cyst infection (fever, flank pain, sepsis)
  - Ruptured intracranial aneurysm (subarachnoid haemorrhage)
  - Acute hypertension (can be severe)
  - Obstructive uropathy (rare, from large cysts compressing ureter)
- **Chronic:**
  - Progressive CKD → ESRD requiring RRT (dialysis/transplant) – median age 58 (PKD1) vs 79 (PKD2)
  - Hypertension – early and often severe
  - Chronic pain – from cyst expansion, pressure
  - Recurrent UTIs
  - Hepatic cysts (co-existent with renal cysts in >80% by age 60)
  - Cardiac valvular abnormalities (mitral valve prolapse)
  - Colonic diverticula (incidence increased)
  - Abdominal wall hernias (from increased intra-abdominal pressure)

### Prognosis
- Median age at ESRD: PKD1 mutation ~58 years; PKD2 mutation ~79 years. Progression slowed by BP control and tolvaptan (if eligible). Life expectancy reduced vs general population (cardiovascular disease main cause of death pre-ESRD; infection/sepsis after RRT).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Start an ACE-I or ARB early** (at first sign of hypertension or microalbuminuria) – renoprotective and reduces left ventricular hypertrophy.
- **Always ask about family history** of brain aneurysm – if positive or personal high risk, refer for screening MRA.
- **Maintain high hydration** (2–3L/day) – reduces vasopressin levels, may slow cyst growth (especially in early-stage disease before eGFR decline).
- **In suspected cyst infection:** treat for 2-4 weeks (longer than standard pyelonephritis) – abscesses often require drainage.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT give NSAIDs** for pain in ADPKD – they reduce renal blood flow and can precipitate AKI, especially in CKD.
- **Do NOT dismiss new severe headache** as “just a migraine” – always CT head to exclude ICA rupture. ADPKD has 5-10% prevalence of IA.
- **Do NOT delay antibiotics** for cyst infection while waiting for imaging – sepsis can progress rapidly. Start empirical IV antibiotics after blood cultures.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Sudden severe headache (worst ever) – call 999
  - Fever >38°C with flank pain – possible cyst infection
  - Visible haematuria with inability to pass urine – clot retention
  - Acute severe flank pain that does not settle – re-evaluate for haemorrhage/stones
  - New confusion, seizure, or focal weakness – ?intracranial bleed
- **Follow-up:** Nephrology clinic within 4-6 weeks for stable patients; routine monitoring BP/eGFR every 6 months. If new diagnosis, refer to renal genetics if considering family screening.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially with fever, hypotension)
  - Sudden GCS drop <13 or seizure (suspected ICA rupture)
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L (sepsis)
  - eGFR decline >30% in 48h (acute on chronic kidney injury)
  - Oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/h for >6h) despite fluid resuscitation
  - Unable to control BP (SBP >180 despite oral therapy)
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 → immediate senior review
  - GCS <8 → call ICU for airway support
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L → sepsis / shock → involve critical care
  - Anuria for >12h → urgent urology and nephrology
